sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (answer key for odd-numbered review questions)

meta.notes: Psychology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch. 15 and Ch. 16 review-questions pages; correct answers from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), odd-numbered review questions only. Ch. 15 Q7 (psbb-x131): the Solutions Guide left this answer blank, so its answer (generalized anxiety disorder) is marked verified-from-source-text (definitionally unambiguous). Live OpenStax pages show CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

hints.prompt: The 'harmful dysfunction' analysis defines a disorder as requiring BOTH of which two elements?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A harmful outcome AND an internal dysfunction

choices.label: D
choices.text: A supportive family AND a stable job

hints.rationale: In Wakefield's model, a disorder requires both harm (a value judgment) and an internal dysfunction.

hints.prompt: The 'harmful' part of the model is a value judgment; the 'dysfunction' part adds which kind of criterion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A more objective, science-based criterion about an internal mechanism

hints.rationale: Dysfunction adds an objective, science-based criterion (an internal mechanism failing) to the value judgment of harm.

hints.prompt: The model locates dysfunction INSIDE the individual. Which two main-question options instead describe EXTERNAL social problems that do not fit 'dysfunction'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'The breakdown of social order in one's community' and 'communication problems in one's immediate family'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'The inability of a psychological mechanism to perform its function' and 'all the above'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'All the above' and 'communication problems in one's family'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'The breakdown of social order' and 'the inability of a mechanism to perform its function'

hints.rationale: Social-order and family-communication problems are external, so they do not fit 'dysfunction' and can be eliminated.

questions.stem: In the harmful dysfunction definition of psychological disorders, dysfunction involves ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: the inability of a psychological mechanism to perform its function

choices.label: B
choices.text: the breakdown of social order in one's community

choices.label: C
choices.text: communication problems in one's immediate family

questions.explanation: In Wakefield's harmful dysfunction model, dysfunction means an internal psychological mechanism is unable to perform the function it evolved to perform.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax (option A printed as 'a psychological mechanism'). correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q1 = A.

hints.prompt: What is the DSM-5 used for?
hints.rationale: The DSM-5 is the standard manual for classifying and diagnosing mental disorders.

hints.prompt: The DSM catalogs 'Mental Disorders.' A title using which word would therefore be a poor fit for it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Medicine,' since the manual is not a general medical text

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Manual,' since it is a reference book

hints.rationale: The DSM concerns mental disorders specifically, not general 'medicine,' so a 'Manual of Medicine' title misdescribes it.

hints.prompt: Two main-question options begin with 'Diseases,' but the DSM is about diagnosing disorders, not cataloging physical diseases. Which two can be eliminated on that basis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Diseases and Statistics Manual of Medicine' and 'Diseases and Symptoms Manual of Mental Disorders'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Diagnosable Standards Manual of Mental Disorders' and 'Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Diseases and Statistics Manual of Medicine' and 'Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Diagnosable Standards Manual of Mental Disorders' and 'Diseases and Symptoms Manual of Mental Disorders'

hints.rationale: The two 'Diseases...' titles misdescribe the manual and can be eliminated.

questions.stem: The letters in the abbreviation DSM-5 stand for ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: Diseases and Statistics Manual of Medicine

choices.label: B
choices.text: Diagnosable Standards Manual of Mental Disorders

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diseases and Symptoms Manual of Mental Disorders

choices.label: D
choices.text: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders

questions.explanation: DSM-5 stands for the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, fifth edition, the standard U.S. classification of mental disorders.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q3 = D.

hints.prompt: In the diathesis-stress model, the word 'diathesis' refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A predisposition or vulnerability (for example, genetic or biological) to a disorder

hints.rationale: A diathesis is a predisposing vulnerability to a disorder.

hints.prompt: In the name 'diathesis-stress model,' the 'stress' component refers to which of the following?
hints.rationale: The 'stress' component refers to adverse or stressful life experiences that interact with the vulnerability.

hints.prompt: Two main-question options describe ONLY internal biology, with no role for life experience. Since the model requires stressors too, which two can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Biochemical factors' and 'chemical imbalances and structural abnormalities in the brain'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Vulnerability and adverse experiences' and 'adverse childhood experiences'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Adverse childhood experiences' and 'chemical imbalances in the brain'

hints.rationale: Those two options omit stress/experience entirely, so they can be eliminated.

questions.stem: The diathesis-stress model presumes that psychopathology results from ________.

choices.label: C
choices.text: chemical imbalances and structural abnormalities in the brain

questions.explanation: The diathesis-stress model holds that psychopathology arises from the interaction of a predisposing vulnerability (diathesis) and stressful or adverse experiences.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q5 = A.

hints.prompt: What is the core feature shared by all anxiety disorders?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excessive fear and/or anxiety and related behavioral disturbances

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of memory for personal events

hints.rationale: Anxiety disorders share excessive fear and/or anxiety.

hints.prompt: Some anxiety disorders involve worry about a SPECIFIC trigger, while others involve pervasive, free-floating worry not tied to any one thing. The pervasive, non-specific type is best described as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic, generalized worry about many things at once

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fear triggered only by social scrutiny

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fear of being unable to escape a place

hints.rationale: Free-floating, non-specific chronic worry describes the generalized type of anxiety.

hints.prompt: Which two of the main-question options are defined by a SPECIFIC trigger (social situations; being trapped or unable to escape) rather than continuous, non-specific worry?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Generalized anxiety disorder and panic disorder

choices.label: D
choices.text: Generalized anxiety disorder and social anxiety disorder

hints.rationale: Social anxiety (social scrutiny) and agoraphobia (inescapable situations) have specific triggers, so they can be eliminated.

questions.stem: In which of the following anxiety disorders is the person in a continuous state of excessive, pointless worry and apprehension?
questions.explanation: Generalized anxiety disorder is characterized by continuous, excessive, and uncontrollable worry and apprehension that is not tied to a specific object or situation.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. The OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide left the Ch. 15 Q7 answer blank; answer (B = generalized anxiety disorder) independently verified from the definitional OpenStax source text, hence marked verified-from-source-text.

hints.prompt: In OCD, a 'compulsion' is best described as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A repetitive behavior or mental act performed to reduce anxiety

choices.label: B
choices.text: A persistent unwanted thought or urge

hints.rationale: A compulsion is a repetitive act (including a mental act) performed to relieve anxiety.

hints.prompt: How does an 'obsession' differ from a 'compulsion'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An obsession is an intrusive thought or urge, whereas a compulsion is a repetitive act done in response

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are two words for the same thing

choices.label: C
choices.text: An obsession is an action and a compulsion is a thought

hints.rationale: Obsessions are intrusive thoughts; compulsions are the repetitive acts done in response.

hints.prompt: Which two of the main-question options are intrusive THOUGHTS (obsessions) rather than repetitive acts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Persistent fear of germs' and 'thoughts of harming a neighbor'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Mentally counting backward from 1,000' and 'persistent fear of germs'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Thoughts of harming a neighbor' and 'mentally counting backward from 1,000'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Falsely believing that a spouse has been cheating' and 'mentally counting backward'

hints.rationale: A fear of germs and thoughts of harm are obsessions (thoughts), not compulsive acts, so they can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Which of the following best illustrates a compulsion?

choices.label: D
choices.text: falsely believing that a spouse has been cheating

questions.explanation: A compulsion is a repetitive behavior or mental act performed to reduce anxiety. Mentally counting backward is a mental compulsion; the other options describe obsessions or delusions.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q9 = A.

hints.prompt: PTSD develops after which of the following?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A chemical imbalance present from birth

hints.rationale: PTSD follows exposure to a traumatic event.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is a recognized cluster of PTSD symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intrusive re-experiencing (memories/flashbacks), avoidance, and heightened arousal

hints.rationale: PTSD symptom clusters include intrusion, avoidance, and hyperarousal.

hints.prompt: This is an 'except' question. Which two of the main-question options are clearly core PTSD symptoms (and therefore NOT the exception)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Intrusive thoughts or memories of a traumatic event' and 'avoidance of things that remind one of a traumatic event'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Jumpiness' and 'physical complaints that cannot be explained medically'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Avoidance of reminders' and 'physical complaints that cannot be explained medically'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Intrusive memories' and 'physical complaints that cannot be explained medically'

hints.rationale: Intrusive memories and avoidance are hallmark PTSD symptoms, so they are not the exception and can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Symptoms of PTSD include all of the following except ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: intrusive thoughts or memories of a traumatic event

choices.label: B
choices.text: avoidance of things that remind one of a traumatic event

choices.label: D
choices.text: physical complaints that cannot be explained medically

questions.explanation: PTSD symptoms include intrusive memories, avoidance, and heightened arousal (jumpiness). Medically unexplained physical complaints are not a core PTSD symptom.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q11 = D.

hints.prompt: Major depressive disorder is characterized primarily by which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Persistent low mood and/or loss of interest or pleasure

hints.rationale: MDD centers on depressed mood and/or loss of interest or pleasure.

hints.prompt: In major depression, changes in concentration and psychomotor activity typically involve which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Trouble concentrating, indecision, and psychomotor agitation or retardation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Racing euphoric thoughts and limitless energy

choices.label: C
choices.text: No changes in thinking or movement at all

hints.rationale: Depression involves impaired concentration and psychomotor changes.

hints.prompt: This is an 'except' question. Which two of the main-question options are genuine depressive symptoms (thus NOT the exception)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Difficulty concentrating and making decisions' and 'loss of interest or pleasure in usual activities'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Periods of extreme elation and euphoria' and 'loss of interest or pleasure'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Psychomotor agitation and retardation' and 'periods of extreme elation and euphoria'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Periods of extreme elation and euphoria' and 'difficulty concentrating'

hints.rationale: Trouble concentrating and loss of interest are classic depressive symptoms, so they are not the exception and can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Common symptoms of major depressive disorder include all of the following except ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: periods of extreme elation and euphoria

choices.label: C
choices.text: loss of interest or pleasure in usual activities

questions.explanation: Periods of extreme elation and euphoria are characteristic of mania (bipolar disorder), not major depressive disorder. The other options are common depressive symptoms.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q13 = A.

hints.prompt: A 'delusion' is best defined as which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A fixed, false belief maintained despite contrary evidence

choices.label: B
choices.text: A false sensory perception, such as hearing voices

choices.label: D
choices.text: An absence of normal emotional expression

hints.rationale: A delusion is a fixed, false belief held despite contrary evidence.

hints.prompt: How does a delusion differ from a hallucination?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A delusion is a false BELIEF, whereas a hallucination is a false PERCEPTION

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are exactly the same phenomenon

choices.label: C
choices.text: A delusion is a perception and a hallucination is a belief

hints.rationale: Delusions are false beliefs; hallucinations are false perceptions.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is a disorder of SPEECH (disorganized, off-track talking) rather than a false belief?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hearing voices that are not there

hints.rationale: Tangentiality is a speech disturbance, not a false belief, so it can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Clifford falsely believes that the police have planted secret cameras in his home to monitor his every movement. Clifford's belief is an example of ________.
questions.explanation: A fixed, false belief held despite contrary evidence (here, a paranoid belief about surveillance) is a delusion, a positive symptom of psychosis.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q15 = A.

hints.prompt: Dissociative disorders involve disruptions in which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Memory, identity, consciousness, or perception, typically linked to psychological stress

hints.rationale: Dissociative disorders disrupt memory, identity, consciousness, or perception, often after stress.

hints.prompt: Dissociative amnesia's memory loss is caused by which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Psychological stress or trauma, not a physical brain injury

hints.rationale: Dissociative amnesia stems from psychological stress, not physical injury, so a physical-injury cause can be eliminated.

hints.prompt: Which option describes DEPERSONALIZATION (feeling detached from oneself) rather than a problem with memory?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Losing the ability to recall personal information after surgery

hints.rationale: 'Feeling detached from the self' is depersonalization, not a memory problem, so it can be eliminated.

questions.explanation: Dissociative amnesia is an inability to recall important personal information, usually following a stressful or traumatic event, that is too extensive to be ordinary forgetting and is not due to physical injury.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q17 = B.

hints.prompt: ADHD is primarily characterized by which of the following?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Fixed, repetitive interests and social-communication deficits

choices.label: D
choices.text: Loss of memory for personal events

hints.rationale: ADHD is defined by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity.

hints.prompt: ADHD symptoms cluster into two domains. Which pair correctly names those domains?
hints.rationale: ADHD's two symptom domains are inattention and hyperactivity-impulsivity.

hints.prompt: This is a 'not a characteristic' question. Which two of the main-question options are clearly ADHD symptoms (inattention or hyperactivity) and thus NOT the exception?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Short attention span' and 'excessive fidgeting and squirming'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Restricted and fixated interest' and 'short attention span'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Difficulty concentrating and distractibility' and 'restricted and fixated interest'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Restricted and fixated interest' and 'excessive fidgeting and squirming'

hints.rationale: Short attention span (inattention) and fidgeting (hyperactivity) are ADHD symptoms, so they are not the exception and can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not a primary characteristic of ADHD?
questions.explanation: Restricted and fixated interests are characteristic of autism spectrum disorder, not ADHD. ADHD is defined by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q19 = C.

hints.prompt: Personality disorders involve which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enduring, inflexible patterns of inner experience and behavior that deviate from cultural expectations

choices.label: B
choices.text: Temporary sadness after a specific loss

hints.rationale: Personality disorders are enduring, inflexible, maladaptive patterns of experience and behavior.

hints.prompt: Borderline personality disorder is marked chiefly by instability in which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mood, self-image, and relationships, along with marked impulsivity

hints.rationale: BPD is characterized by instability in mood, self-image, and relationships, plus impulsivity.

hints.prompt: Two of the main-question options describe OTHER personality disorders: attention-seeking (histrionic) and accomplishing goals through cruelty (antisocial). Which two are those?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Try to be the center of attention' and 'tend to accomplish goals through cruelty'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Are impulsive and unpredictable' and 'are shy and withdrawn'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Are shy and withdrawn' and 'try to be the center of attention'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Are impulsive and unpredictable' and 'tend to accomplish goals through cruelty'

hints.rationale: Center-of-attention (histrionic) and cruelty (antisocial) describe other disorders, so they can be eliminated.

questions.stem: People with borderline personality disorder often ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: try to be the center of attention

choices.label: D
choices.text: tend to accomplish goals through cruelty

questions.explanation: Borderline personality disorder is characterized by instability in mood, self-image, and relationships, along with marked impulsivity and unpredictability.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q21 = C.

hints.prompt: The 'moral treatment' movement in mental health advocated which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Humane, compassionate care for people with mental illness

choices.label: B
choices.text: Punishment and exorcism of the mentally ill

hints.rationale: Moral treatment promoted humane, compassionate care.

hints.prompt: Philippe Pinel is historically remembered for which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Advocating humane, reform-minded treatment of the mentally ill

hints.rationale: Pinel advocated humane treatment, so he DID support it and can be eliminated.

hints.prompt: The question asks who does NOT support humane treatment. If at least one listed figure clearly WAS a humane-treatment reformer, can 'All of the above' be the correct answer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because some listed figures did support humane treatment

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, all listed figures opposed humane treatment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, the reformers opposed humane care

choices.label: D
choices.text: It cannot be determined at all

hints.rationale: Because at least one listed figure supported humane treatment, 'All of the above' cannot be correct and can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Who of the following does not support the humane and improved treatment of people with mental illness?
questions.explanation: Pinel and Dix were reformers who advocated humane treatment of people with mental illness. Medieval priests, who often attributed mental illness to demonic possession, did not.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q1 = B.

hints.prompt: In mental-health care, 'voluntary treatment' means which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The person chooses to seek treatment of their own free will

choices.label: B
choices.text: Treatment that a court orders a person to attend

choices.label: D
choices.text: Treatment that is against the law

hints.rationale: Voluntary treatment is sought freely by the person.

hints.prompt: How is 'involuntary treatment' different from 'voluntary treatment'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Involuntary treatment is imposed on a person who has not chosen it, unlike voluntary treatment

choices.label: B
choices.text: Involuntary treatment is always chosen freely by the patient

choices.label: C
choices.text: They mean exactly the same thing

choices.label: D
choices.text: Involuntary treatment applies only to physical illness

hints.rationale: Involuntary treatment is imposed on a person who did not choose it, unlike voluntary treatment.

hints.prompt: Court-ordered treatment is clearly NOT chosen freely. Which option is the direct OPPOSITE of court-ordered treatment and can be eliminated first?
hints.rationale: 'Voluntary' (freely chosen) is the opposite of court-ordered treatment, so it can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Joey was convicted of domestic violence. As part of his sentence, the judge has ordered that he attend therapy for anger management. This is considered ________ treatment.
questions.explanation: Court-ordered treatment that a person does not choose for themselves is classified as involuntary treatment.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q3 = A.

hints.prompt: Which therapy focuses on changing observable BEHAVIOR through conditioning (rewards, punishments, and associations)?
hints.rationale: Behavior therapy changes behavior through conditioning.

hints.prompt: Client-centered therapy is built primarily around which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The therapist's empathy and unconditional positive regard

hints.rationale: Client-centered therapy centers on empathy and unconditional positive regard.

hints.prompt: The main question is about a therapy focused on THOUGHTS. Which two listed therapies are NOT primarily about changing thoughts?
hints.rationale: Behavior therapy (conditioning) and client-centered therapy (empathy) are not primarily about changing thoughts, so they can be eliminated.

questions.stem: The idea behind ________ is that how you think determines how you feel and act.
questions.explanation: Cognitive therapy is based on the premise that thoughts shape feelings and behavior, so changing maladaptive thinking changes how a person feels and acts.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q5 = A.

hints.prompt: Psychoanalysis (Freudian therapy) mainly aims to do which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bring unconscious conflicts into conscious awareness

choices.label: B
choices.text: Change behavior through rewards and punishments

hints.rationale: Psychoanalysis seeks to uncover unconscious material.

hints.prompt: Which technique belongs to BEHAVIOR therapy (not psychoanalysis) and is used to reduce phobias by pairing relaxation with feared stimuli?
hints.rationale: Systematic desensitization is a behavior-therapy technique, so that pairing can be eliminated.

hints.prompt: 'Active listening' is a hallmark technique of which therapy?
hints.rationale: Active listening is a client-centered technique, so the active-listening option is not the psychoanalytic 'say whatever comes to mind' technique and can be set aside.

questions.stem: Clay is in a therapy session. The therapist asks him to relax and say whatever comes to his mind at the moment. This therapist is using ________, which is a technique of ________.
questions.explanation: Asking a client to relax and say whatever comes to mind is free association, a core technique of psychoanalysis.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q7 = D.

hints.prompt: In a course of therapy, WHEN does an 'intake' typically occur?

choices.label: A
choices.text: At the very first meeting with a new client

choices.label: B
choices.text: At the final session that ends therapy

hints.rationale: An intake is the initial session with a new client.

hints.prompt: Descriptions of working on a couple's relationship or a family's boundaries and structure refer to which kind of therapy, rather than an intake?
hints.rationale: Couple- and family-focused work describes couples/family therapy, distinguishing it from an intake.

hints.prompt: Which two of the main-question options describe ongoing COUPLE or FAMILY therapy work rather than an initial information-gathering session?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Meets with a couple to see how their backgrounds affect their relationship' and 'examines the family's boundaries and structure'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Gathers information about the presenting problem and insurance' and 'informs the client about fees and confidentiality'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Gathers information about the presenting problem' and 'examines the family's boundaries and structure'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Informs the client about confidentiality' and 'meets with a couple about their relationship'

hints.rationale: The couple and family options describe couples/family therapy, not an intake, so they can be eliminated.

choices.label: A
choices.text: The therapist gathers specific information to address the client's immediate needs such as the presenting problem, the client's support system, and insurance status. The therapist informs the client about confidentiality, fees, and what to expect in a therapy session.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The therapist guides what happens in the therapy session and designs a detailed approach to resolving each member's presenting problem.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The therapist meets with a couple to help them see how their individual backgrounds, beliefs, and actions are affecting their relationship.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The therapist examines and discusses with the family the boundaries and structure of the family: For example, who makes the rules, who sleeps in the bed with whom, and how decisions are made.

questions.explanation: An intake is the therapist's first meeting with a client to gather information about presenting problems, support systems, and logistics (confidentiality, fees, expectations).

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q9 = A.

hints.prompt: The prefix 'co-' (as in coexist and co-occur) means which of the following?
hints.rationale: 'Co-' means together, a clue for a term describing disorders occurring together.

hints.prompt: Two of the main-question options are NOT terms for having multiple co-occurring diagnoses (one is a relationship pattern; one is not a real clinical term). Which two?
hints.rationale: 'Codependency' is a relationship dynamic and 'bi-morbid disorder' is not a real term, so both can be eliminated.

hints.prompt: A single term for having two or more disorders at the same time uses the 'co-' (together) prefix. Such disorders are described as existing in which way?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Together, at the same time, in one person

choices.label: B
choices.text: One after another across a lifetime

hints.rationale: Co-occurring disorders exist together, at the same time, in one person.

questions.stem: When an individual has two or more diagnoses, which often includes a substance-related diagnosis and another psychiatric diagnosis, this is known as ________.
questions.explanation: When two or more disorders occur together in the same person, they are described as comorbid disorders (comorbidity).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q11 = B.

hints.prompt: Which perspective explains behavior mainly through brain chemistry and genetics, in contrast to the sociocultural perspective?
hints.rationale: The biological perspective focuses on brain chemistry and genetics, unlike the sociocultural perspective.

hints.prompt: The sociocultural perspective looks at the BROAD context a person lives in. Two of the main-question options name narrow individual demographics rather than that broad context. Which two?
hints.rationale: Education and age are narrow demographics, not the broad sociocultural context, so they can be set aside.

hints.prompt: Socioeconomic status is one specific factor. Compared with income status alone, the context the sociocultural perspective emphasizes is which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Broader, encompassing shared customs, values, and heritage

hints.rationale: The sociocultural context is broader than income status alone, so socioeconomic status by itself is too narrow.

questions.stem: The sociocultural perspective looks at you, your behaviors, and your symptoms in the context of your ________.
questions.explanation: The sociocultural perspective interprets a person's behavior and symptoms within the context of their culture and background.

questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 16 Q13 = C.
